[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1987–1988")]
Naturally aspirated engines, now at up to 3.5L, were reintroduced for the 1987 season[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-39) with a plan to retain the option for up to 1.5L supercharged engines for two seasons before their eventual ban in 1989. The 1987 technical regulations now specified that the only acceptable means of supercharging was via the use of one or more turbochargers.[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-40)

The FIA regulations limited boost pressure, to 4 bar in qualifying in 1987 for 1.5 L [turbosupercharged](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger"). Fuel tank sizes were further reduced in size to 150 litres for turbocharged cars to limit the amount of boost used in a race. These seasons were still dominated by turbocharged engines, the [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_Racing_F1 "Honda Racing F1") [RA167E](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_RA16_engine "Honda RA16 engine") V6 of [Nelson Piquet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nelson_Piquet "Nelson Piquet") winning the [1987 Formula One season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1987_Formula_One_season "1987 Formula One season") in a [Williams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WilliamsF1 "WilliamsF1") also winning the

season") in a [Williams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WilliamsF1 "WilliamsF1") also winning the constructors championship, followed by the [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_Racing_F1 "Honda Racing F1") [RA168E](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_RA16_engine "Honda RA16 engine") V6 of [Ayrton Senna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayrton_Senna "Ayrton Senna") winning the [1988 Formula One season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1988_Formula_One_season "1988 Formula One season") in a [McLaren](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLaren "McLaren") who likewise won the constructors championship.

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Honda_RA168E_engine_rear_Honda_Collection_Hall.jpg/250px-Honda_RA168E_engine_rear_Honda_Collection_Hall.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Honda_RA168E_engine_rear_Honda_Collection_Hall.jpg)A 1988 [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_F1 "Honda F1") [RA168E](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_RA16_engine "Honda RA16 engine") turbocharged V6 engine

The rest of the grid was powered by the [Ford](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company#Formula_One "Ford Motor Company") GBA V6 turbo in [Benetton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benetton_Formula "Benetton Formula"), with the only naturally aspirated engine, the DFV-derived Ford-Cosworth DFZ 3.5 L V8 outputting 575 hp (429 kW) in [Tyrrell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tyrrell_Racing "Tyrrell Racing"), [Lola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lola_Racing_Cars "Lola Racing Cars"), [AGS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automobiles_Gonfaronnaises_Sportives "Automobiles Gonfaronnaises Sportives"), [March](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/March_Engineering "March Engineering") and [Coloni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzo_Coloni_Racing_Car_Systems "Enzo Coloni Racing Car

and [Coloni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzo_Coloni_Racing_Car_Systems "Enzo Coloni Racing Car Systems").[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-statsf1-41) For the 1988 season, [Benetton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benetton_Formula "Benetton Formula") adopted the heavily revised 3.5L Ford [DFR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosworth_DFR "Cosworth DFR") V8 while [Williams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WilliamsF1 "WilliamsF1"), having lost their supply of Honda turbocharged engines, adopted a 3.5L [Judd](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judd_\(engine\) "Judd \(engine\)") V8 for the 1988 season.

In [1988](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1988_Formula_One_World_Championship "1988 Formula One World Championship"), six teams – McLaren, Ferrari, Lotus, Arrows, Osella and Zakspeed – continued with turbocharged engines, now limited to 2.5 bar. Honda's V6 turbo, the RA168E, which produced 685 hp (511 kW) at 12,300 rpm in qualifying,[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-42) powered the [McLaren MP4/4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLaren_MP4/4 "McLaren MP4/4") with which [Ayrton Senna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayrton_Senna "Ayrton Senna") and [Alain Prost](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alain_Prost "Alain Prost") won fifteen of the sixteen races between them. The Italian Grand Prix was won by [Gerhard Berger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerhard_Berger

The Italian Grand Prix was won by [Gerhard Berger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerhard_Berger "Gerhard Berger") in the [Ferrari F1/87/88C](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrari_F1/87 "Ferrari F1/87"), powered by the team's own V6 turbo, the 033E, with about 720 hp (537 kW) at 12,000 rpm in qualifying and 620 hp (462 kW) at 12,000 rpm in races.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-43) The Honda turbo also powered Lotus's [100T](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lotus_100T "Lotus 100T"), while Arrows continued with the Megatron-badged BMW turbo, Osella continued with the Alfa Romeo V8 (now badged as an Osella) and Zakspeed continued with their own straight-4 turbo. All the other teams used naturally aspirated 3.5 L V8 engines: Benetton used the Cosworth DFR, which

the other teams used naturally aspirated 3.5 L V8 engines: Benetton used the Cosworth DFR, which produced 585 hp (436 kW) at 11,000 rpm;[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-:0-44) Williams, March and Ligier used the [Judd](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judd_\(engine\) "Judd \(engine\)") CV, producing 600 hp (447 kW);[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-45) and the rest of the grid used the previous year's 575 hp (429 kW) Cosworth DFZ.

### 1989–1994
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1989–1994")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Paris_-_Retromobile_2012_-_Renault_moteur_F1_V10_-_001.jpg/250px-Paris_-_Retromobile_2012_-_Renault_moteur_F1_V10_-_001.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Paris_-_Retromobile_2012_-_Renault_moteur_F1_V10_-_001.jpg)A 1990 [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_F1 "Renault F1") RS2 V10 engine

Turbochargers were banned from the [1989 Formula One season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1989_Formula_One_season "1989 Formula One season"), leaving only a naturally aspirated 3.5 L formula. Honda was still dominant with their RA109E 72° V10 giving 685 hp (511 kW) @ 13,500 rpm on [McLaren](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLaren_\(racing\) "McLaren \(racing\)") cars, enabling Prost to win the championship in front of his teammate Senna. Behind were the [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_F1 "Renault F1") RS1-powered Williams, a 67° V10 giving 650 hp (485 kW) @ 12,500 rpm and the Ferrari with its 035/5 65° V12 giving 660 hp (492 kW) at 13,000 rpm. Behind, the grid was powered mainly by [Ford Cosworth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford_Cosworth "Ford Cosworth") DFR V8 giving

by [Ford Cosworth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford_Cosworth "Ford Cosworth") DFR V8 giving 620 hp (462 kW) @ 10,750 rpm except for a few Judd CV V8 in Lotus, Brabham and [EuroBrun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EuroBrun "EuroBrun") cars, and two oddballs: the 620 hp (460 kW) [Lamborghini](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamborghini "Lamborghini") 3512 80° V12 powering Lola, and the 560 hp (420 kW) [Yamaha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yamaha_Motor_Company "Yamaha Motor Company") OX88 75° V8 in Zakspeed cars. Ford started to try its new design, the 75° V8 HBA1 with Benetton.

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/W12_Engine.jpg/250px-W12_Engine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:W12_Engine.jpg)A 1990 W12 3.5 Formula One engine from the [Life F1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Life_F1 "Life F1") car

The [1990 Formula One season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1990_Formula_One_World_Championship "1990 Formula One World Championship") was again dominated by Honda in McLarens with the 690 hp (515 kW) @ 13,500 rpm RA100E powering [Ayrton Senna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayrton_Senna "Ayrton Senna") and [Gerhard Berger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerhard_Berger "Gerhard Berger") ahead of the 680 hp (507 kW) @ 12,750 rpm Ferrari Tipo 036 of [Alain Prost](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alain_Prost "Alain Prost") and [Nigel Mansell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nigel_Mansell "Nigel Mansell"). Behind them the Ford HBA4 for Benetton and Renault RS2 for Williams with 660 hp (492 kW) @ 12,800 rpm were leading the pack powered by Ford DFR and Judd CV engines. The exceptions were the

@ 12,800 rpm were leading the pack powered by Ford DFR and Judd CV engines. The exceptions were the Lamborghini 3512 in Lola and Lotus, and the new Judd EV 76° V8 giving 640 hp (477 kW) @ 12,500 rpm in [Leyton House](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leyton_House_Racing "Leyton House Racing") and Brabham cars. The two new contenders were the [Life](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Life_\(Racing_Team\) "Life \(Racing Team\)") which built for themselves an F35 W12 with three four cylinders banks @ 60°, and [Subaru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subaru "Subaru") giving [Coloni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzo_Coloni_Racing_Car_Systems "Enzo Coloni Racing Car Systems") a 1235 flat-12 from [Motori Moderni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Motori_Moderni "Motori Moderni")

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Honda_RA121E_engine_Honda_Collection_Hall.jpg/250px-Honda_RA121E_engine_Honda_Collection_Hall.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Honda_RA121E_engine_Honda_Collection_Hall.jpg)A 1991 [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_F1 "Honda F1") RA121E V12 engine

Honda was still leading the [1991 Formula One season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1991_Formula_One_season "1991 Formula One season") in Senna's McLaren with the 725–780 hp (541–582 kW) @ 13,500–14,500 rpm 60° V12 RA121E, just ahead of the Renault RS3 powered Williams benefiting from 700–750 hp (520–560 kW) @ 12,500–13,000 rpm. Ferrari was behind with its Tipo 037, a new 65° V12 giving 710 hp (529 kW) @ 13,800 rpm also powering [Minardi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minardi "Minardi"), just ahead the Ford HBA4/5/6 in Benetton and Jordan cars. Behind, Tyrrell was using the previous Honda RA109E, Judd introduced its new GV with [Dallara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dallara "Dallara") leaving the previous EV to Lotus, Yamaha were giving its 660 hp (492 kW) OX99 70° V12 to Brabham,

leaving the previous EV to Lotus, Yamaha were giving its 660 hp (492 kW) OX99 70° V12 to Brabham, Lamborghini engines were used by [Modena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modena_\(racing_team\) "Modena \(racing team\)") and Ligier. [Ilmor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ilmor "Ilmor") introduced its LH10, a 680 hp (507 kW) @ 13,000 rpm V10 which eventually became the [Mercedes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_HighPerformanceEngines "Mercedes-Benz HighPerformanceEngines") with Leyton House and [Porsche](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porsche_in_Formula_One "Porsche in Formula One") sourced a little successful [3512](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porsche_3512 "Porsche 3512") V12 to [Footwork Arrows](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Footwork_Arrows "Footwork Arrows"); the rest of the
[Footwork Arrows](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Footwork_Arrows "Footwork Arrows"); the rest of the field was Ford DFR powered.[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-46)

In 1992, the [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_Sport "Renault Sport") engines became dominant, even more so following the departure from the sport of [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda "Honda") at the end of 1992. The 3.5 L Renault V10 engines powering the Williams F1 team produced a power output between 750–820 bhp (559–611 kW; 760–831 PS) @ 13,000–14,300 rpm toward the end of the 3.5 L naturally aspirated era, between 1992 and 1994. Renault-engined cars won the last three consecutive world constructors' championships of the 3.5 L formula era with [Williams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WilliamsF1 "WilliamsF1") (1992–1994).[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-47)

The [Peugeot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peugeot_Sport "Peugeot Sport") A4 [V10](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V10_engine "V10 engine"), used by the [McLaren](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLaren "McLaren") Formula One team in 1994, initially developed 700 bhp (522 kW; 710 PS) @ 14,250 rpm. It was later further developed into the A6, which produced even more power, developing 760 bhp (567 kW; 771 PS) @ 14,500 rpm.

The _[EC Zetec-R](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford-Cosworth_EC_/_ED_engine "Ford-Cosworth EC / ED engine")_ [V8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine"), which powered the championship-winning [Benetton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benetton_Formula "Benetton Formula") team and [Michael Schumacher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Schumacher "Michael Schumacher") in 1994, produced between 730–750 bhp (544–559 kW; 740–760 PS) @ 14,500 rpm.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-48)

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Ferrari_412_T1_engine.jpg/250px-Ferrari_412_T1_engine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ferrari_412_T1_engine.jpg)A 1994 [Ferrari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari") Tipo 043 3.5 V12 engine; the most powerful 3.5-litre engine in F1 history

By the end of the 1994 season, Ferrari's _Tipo 043_ [V12](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V12_engine "V12 engine") was putting out around 850 hp (634 kW)[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-49) @ 15,800 rpm, which is to date the most powerful naturally aspirated V12 engine ever used in Formula One. This was also the most powerful engine of 3.5-litre engine regulation era, before a reduction in engine capacity to 3 litres in 1995.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-50)
### 1995–2005
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1995–2005")]

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ferrari_V12_Formula_One_racing_engine.jpg/250px-Ferrari_V12_Formula_One_racing_engine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ferrari_V12_Formula_One_racing_engine.jpg)A Ferrari Tipo 044/1 3.0-litre V12 F1 engine (1995) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Ferrari_054_V10_engine.jpg/250px-Ferrari_054_V10_engine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ferrari_054_V10_engine.jpg)A 2004 [Ferrari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari") model 053 V10 engine of the Ferrari F2004

This era used a 3.0 L formula, with the power range varying (depending on engine tuning) between 600 hp (447 kW) and 1,000 hp (746 kW), between 13,000 rpm and 20,000 rpm, and from eight to twelve cylinders. Despite [engine displacement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Engine_displacement "Engine displacement") being reduced from 3.5 L, power figures and RPMs still managed to climb. Renault was the initial dominant engine supplier from 1995 until 1997, winning the first three world championships with Williams and Benetton in this era. The championship-winning 1995 [Benetton B195](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benetton_B195 "Benetton B195") produced a power output of 675 hp (503.3 kW) @ 15,200 rpm, and the 1996 championship-winning [Williams FW18](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Williams_FW18

rpm, and the 1996 championship-winning [Williams FW18](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Williams_FW18 "Williams FW18") produced 700 hp (522.0 kW) @ 16,000 rpm; both from a shared [Renault RS8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_RS_engine "Renault RS engine") 3.0 L [V10 engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V10_engine "V10 engine").[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-51)[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-52) The 1997 championship-winning [FW19](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Williams_FW19 "Williams FW19") produced between 730–760 hp (544.4–566.7 kW) @ 16,000 rpm, from its Renault RS9B 3.0 L V10. Ferrari's last V12 engine, the _[Tipo 044/1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrari_412_T2 "Ferrari 412 T2")_ , was used in

the _[Tipo 044/1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrari_412_T2 "Ferrari 412 T2")_ , was used in [1995](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1995_Formula_One_World_Championship "1995 Formula One World Championship"). The engine's design was largely influenced by major regulation changes imposed by the [FIA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FIA "FIA") after the dreadful events during the year before: the V12 engine was reduced from 3.5 to 3.0 litres. The 3.0-litre engine produced around 700 hp (522 kW) 17,000 rpm in race trim; but was reportedly capable of producing up to 760 hp (567 kW) in its highest state of tune for qualification mode.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-53) Between 1995 and 2000, cars using this 3.0 L engine formula, imposed by the

Between 1995 and 2000, cars using this 3.0 L engine formula, imposed by the [FIA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FIA "FIA"), produced a constant power range (depending on engine type and tuning), varying between 600 hp and 815 hp. Most Formula One cars during the [1997](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1997_Formula_One_World_Championship "1997 Formula One World Championship") season comfortably produced a consistent power output of between 665–760 hp (495.9–566.7 kW), depending on whether a [V8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine") or [V10 engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V10_engine "V10 engine") configuration was used.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-54) From 1998 to 2000 it was Mercedes' power that ruled, giving [Mika

From 1998 to 2000 it was Mercedes' power that ruled, giving [Mika Häkkinen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mika_H%C3%A4kkinen "Mika Häkkinen") two world championships. The 1999 [McLaren MP4/14](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLaren_MP4/14 "McLaren MP4/14") produced between 785 and 810 hp @ 17,000 rpm. [Ferrari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari") gradually improved their engine. In [1996](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1996_Formula_One_World_Championship "1996 Formula One World Championship"), they changed from their traditional [V12 engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V12_engine "V12 engine") to a smaller and lighter V10 engine. They preferred reliability to power, losing out to Mercedes in terms of outright power initially. Ferrari's first V10 engine, in

power, losing out to Mercedes in terms of outright power initially. Ferrari's first V10 engine, in 1996, produced 715 hp (533 kW) @ 15,550 rpm,[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-55) down on power from their most powerful 3.5 L V12 (in 1994), which produced over 830 hp (619 kW) @ 15,800 rpm, but up on power from their last 3.0 L V12 (in 1995), which produced 700 hp (522 kW) @ 17,000 rpm. At the [1998 Japanese GP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1998_Japanese_Grand_Prix "1998 Japanese Grand Prix"), Ferrari's 047D engine spec was said to produce over 800 bhp (600 kW), and from 2000 onward, they were never short of power or reliability. To keep costs down, the 3.0 L V10 engine configuration was made fully mandatory for all teams in 2000 so that engine builders

L V10 engine configuration was made fully mandatory for all teams in 2000 so that engine builders would not develop and experiment with other configurations.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-56) The V10 configuration had been the most popular since the banning of [turbocharged](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbocharged "Turbocharged") engines in 1989, and no other configuration had been used since 1998.

[BMW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BMW_in_Formula_One#Williams_\(2000%E2%80%932005\) "BMW in Formula One") started supplying its engines to Williams from 2000. The engine was very reliable in the first season though slightly short of power compared to Ferrari and Mercedes units. The [BMW E41](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BMW_E41_/_P80_engine "BMW E41 / P80 engine")-powered [Williams FW22](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Williams_FW22 "Williams FW22") produced around 810 hp @ 17,500 rpm, during the 2000 season.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-57) BMW went straight forward with its engine development. The P81, used during the 2001 season, was able to hit 17,810 rpm. Unfortunately, reliability was a large issue with several blowups during the season.

The BMW P82, the engine used by the BMW WilliamsF1 Team in 2002, had hit a peak speed of 19,050 rpm in its final evolutionary stage. It was also the first engine in the 3.0 litre V10-era to break through the 19,000 rpm wall, during the [2002 Italian Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2002_Italian_Grand_Prix "2002 Italian Grand Prix")'s qualifying.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-58) BMW's P83 engine used in 2003 season managed an impressive 19,200 rpm and cleared the 900 bhp (670 kW) mark, at around 940 bhp, and weighs less than 200 lb (91 kg).[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-59)[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-60) [Honda's _RA003E_

[Honda's _RA003E_ V10](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_V10_engine "Honda V10 engine") also cleared the 900 bhp (670 kW) mark at the [2003 Canadian Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2003_Canadian_Grand_Prix "2003 Canadian Grand Prix").[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-61)

In 2005, no more than 5 valves per cylinder were permitted.[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-62) Also, the FIA introduced new regulations limiting each car to one engine per two Grand Prix weekends, putting the emphasis on increased reliability. In spite of this, power outputs continued to rise. Mercedes engines had about 930 bhp (690 kW) in this season. [Cosworth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth"), [Mercedes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercedes_AMG_High_Performance_Powertrains "Mercedes AMG High Performance Powertrains"), [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_in_Formula_One "Renault in Formula One"), and [Ferrari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari") engines all produced around 900 bhp (670 kW) to 940 bhp (700 kW)

"Ferrari") engines all produced around 900 bhp (670 kW) to 940 bhp (700 kW) @ 19,000 rpm.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-63) [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda "Honda") had over 965 bhp (720 kW).[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-64)[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-65) The [BMW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BMW "BMW") engine made over 950 bhp (710 kW).[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-66)[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-67) Toyota engines had over 1,000 bhp (750 kW), according to [Toyota Motorsport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toyota_Racing_\(Formula_One_team\) "Toyota Racing \(Formula One team\)")'s executive

"Toyota Racing \(Formula One team\)")'s executive Vice President, Yoshiaki Kinoshita.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-68) However, for reliability and longevity purposes, this power figure may have been detuned to around 960 bhp (720 kW) for races.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-69)

### 2006–2013
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2006–2013")]
[![Renault RS26 engine 2006](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Renault_RS26_engine_2006.jpg/250px-Renault_RS26_engine_2006.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Renault_RS26_engine_2006.jpg)A [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_in_Formula_One "Renault in Formula One") [RS26](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_RS_engine "Renault RS engine") 2.4 V8 engine (2006)

For 2006, the engines had to be 90° [V8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine") of 2.4 litres maximum capacity with a circular bore of 98 mm (3.9 in) maximum, which implies a 39.75 mm (1.565 in) stroke at maximum bore. The engines must have two inlet and two exhaust [valves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poppet_valve "Poppet valve") per cylinder, be [naturally aspirated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naturally-aspirated "Naturally-aspirated") and have a 95 kg (209 lb) minimum weight. The previous year's engines with a rev-limiter were permitted for 2006 and 2007 for teams who were unable to acquire a V8 engine, with [Scuderia Toro Rosso](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scuderia_Toro_Rosso "Scuderia Toro Rosso") using a Cosworth V10, after Red Bull's takeover of the former

"Scuderia Toro Rosso") using a Cosworth V10, after Red Bull's takeover of the former [Minardi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minardi "Minardi") team did not include the new engines.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-tech-70) The 2006 season saw the highest rev limits in the history of Formula One, at well over 20,000 rpm; before a 19,000 rpm mandatory rev limiter was implemented for all competitors in 2007. Cosworth was able to achieve just over 20,000 rpm with their V8,[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-71) and Renault around 20,500 rpm. Honda did the same, albeit only on the dynamometer.

Pre-cooling air before it enters the cylinders, injection of any substance other than air and fuel into the cylinders, variable-geometry [intake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intake "Intake") and [exhaust systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exhaust_system "Exhaust system"), and [variable valve timing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variable_valve_timing "Variable valve timing") were forbidden. Each cylinder could have only one [fuel injector](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuel_injector "Fuel injector") and a single plug [spark ignition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spark_ignition "Spark ignition"). Separate starting devices were used to start engines in the pits and on the grid. The crankcase and cylinder block had to be made of cast or wrought

in the pits and on the grid. The crankcase and cylinder block had to be made of cast or wrought [aluminium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") alloys. The crankshaft and camshafts had to be made from an [iron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron "Iron") alloy, pistons from an aluminium alloy, and valves from alloys based on [iron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron "Iron"), [nickel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nickel "Nickel"), [cobalt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt") or [titanium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"). These restrictions were in place to reduce development costs on the engines.[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-72)

[![Ferrari 056 engine \(2007\) rear Museo Ferrari](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Ferrari_056_engine_%282007%29_rear_Museo_Ferrari.jpg/250px-Ferrari_056_engine_%282007%29_rear_Museo_Ferrari.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ferrari_056_engine_\(2007\)_rear_Museo_Ferrari.jpg)A [Ferrari _Tipo 056_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrari_V8_F1_engine "Ferrari V8 F1 engine") 2.4 L V8 engine

The reduction in capacity was designed to give a power reduction of around 20% from the three-litre engines, to reduce the increasing speeds of Formula One cars. Despite this, in many cases the performance of the car improved. In 2006 [Toyota F1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toyota_F1 "Toyota F1") announced an approximate 740 hp (552 kW) output at 18,000 rpm for its new RVX-06 engine,[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-73) but real figures are of course difficult to obtain. Most cars from this period (2006–2008) produced a regular power output of approximately between 720 and 800 hp @ 19,000 rpm (over 20,000 rpm for the [2006](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2006_Formula_One_World_Championship "2006 Formula One World Championship") season).[_[citation
"2006 Formula One World Championship") season).[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

The engine specification was frozen in 2007 to keep development costs down. The engines which were used in the [2006 Japanese Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2006_Japanese_Grand_Prix "2006 Japanese Grand Prix") were used for the 2007 and 2008 seasons and they were limited to 19,000 rpm. In 2009 the limit was reduced to 18,000 rpm with each driver allowed to use a maximum of 8 engines over the season. Any driver needing an additional engine is penalised 10 places on the starting grid for the first race the engine is used. This increases the importance of reliability, although the effect is only seen towards the end of the season. Certain design changes intended to improve engine reliability may be carried out with permission from the FIA. This has led to some engine

engine reliability may be carried out with permission from the FIA. This has led to some engine manufacturers, notably Ferrari and Mercedes, exploiting this ability by making design changes which not only improve reliability but also boost engine power output as a side effect. As the Mercedes engine was proven to be the strongest, re-equalisations of engines were allowed by the FIA to allow other manufacturers to match the power.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-74)

2009 saw the exit of Honda from Formula One. The team was acquired by [Ross Brawn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ross_Brawn "Ross Brawn"), creating [Brawn GP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brawn_GP "Brawn GP") and the [BGP 001](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brawn_BGP_001 "Brawn BGP 001"). With the absence of the Honda engine, Brawn GP retrofitted the Mercedes engine to the BGP 001 chassis. The newly branded team won both the Constructors' Championship and the Drivers' Championship from better-known and better-established contenders Ferrari, McLaren-Mercedes, and Renault.

[Cosworth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth"), absent since the end of the [2006 season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2006_Formula_One_season "2006 Formula One season"), returned in 2010. New teams [Lotus Racing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Team_Lotus_\(2010%E2%80%9311\) "Team Lotus \(2010–11\)"), [HRT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispania_Racing_F1_Team "Hispania Racing F1 Team"), and [Virgin Racing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virgin_Racing "Virgin Racing"), along with the established [Williams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WilliamsF1 "WilliamsF1"), used this engine. The season also saw the withdrawal of the [BMW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BMW_in_Formula_One "BMW in Formula One") and [Toyota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toyota_Racing_\(Formula_One_team\)

"BMW in Formula One") and [Toyota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toyota_Racing_\(Formula_One_team\) "Toyota Racing \(Formula One team\)") engines, as the car companies withdrew from Formula One due to the [Great Recession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession").[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-GPE-75)

In 2009, constructors were allowed to use [kinetic energy recovery systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinetic_energy_recovery_system "Kinetic energy recovery system") (KERS), also called [regenerative brakes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regenerative_brake "Regenerative brake"). Energy can either be stored as mechanical energy (as in a flywheel) or as electrical energy (as in a battery or supercapacitor), with a maximum power of 81 hp (60 kW; 82 PS) deployed by an [electric motor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor"), for a little over 6 seconds per lap. Four teams used it at some point in the season: Ferrari, Renault, BMW, and McLaren.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-76)

Although KERS was still legal in F1 in the 2010 season, all the teams agreed not to use it. KERS returned for the 2011 season, when only three teams elected not to use it. For the 2012 season, only [Marussia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marussia_F1 "Marussia F1") and HRT raced without KERS, and in 2013 all teams on the grid had KERS. From 2010 to 2013 cars have a regular power of 700–800 hp, averaging around 750 hp @ 18,000 rpm.[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-77)[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-78)
### 2014–2025
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2014–2025")]

The FIA announced a change from the 2.4-litre [V8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine"), introducing 1.6-litre [V6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V6_engine "V6 engine") [hybrid engines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hybrid_vehicle_drivetrain#Hybrid_vehicle_operation_modes "Hybrid vehicle drivetrain") (more than one power source) for the [2014](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2014_Formula_One_World_Championship "2014 Formula One World Championship") season. The new regulations allow kinetic and heat [energy recovery systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_recovery "Energy recovery").[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-79) Forced induction was now allowed – either [turbochargers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger"),

now allowed – either [turbochargers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger"), which last appeared in [1988](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1988_Formula_One_World_Championship "1988 Formula One World Championship"), or [superchargers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supercharger "Supercharger") – with all constructors opting to use a turbocharger. Instead of limiting the boost level, the regulations introduced a fuel flow restriction at 100 kg of petrol per hour maximum. The engines sounded very different from the previous formula, due to the lower rev limit (15,000 rpm) and the turbocharger. The introduction of these systems in 2014 significantly altered race strategies and team budgets. Energy recovery and deployment strategies became critical factors in race outcomes. On

budgets. Energy recovery and deployment strategies became critical factors in race outcomes. On top of that the complexity and cost of developing these hybrid systems led to substantial increases in team spending. This financial strain contributed to the FIA's decision to implement a cost cap starting in the 2021 season, aiming to ensure a more level playing field among teams.[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-80)

The new formula for turbocharged engines have their efficiency improved through [turbo-compounding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbo-compound "Turbo-compound") by recovering energy from exhaust gases.[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-JAE-81) The original proposal for four-cylinder turbocharged engines was not welcomed by the racing teams, in particular Ferrari. [Adrian Newey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrian_Newey "Adrian Newey") stated during the [2011 European Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_European_Grand_Prix "2011 European Grand Prix") that the change to a V6 enables teams to carry the engine as a [stressed member](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stressed_member_engine "Stressed member engine"), whereas an inline-4 would have

"Stressed member engine"), whereas an inline-4 would have required a space frame. A compromise was reached, allowing V6 forced induction engines instead.[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-JAE-81) The engines rarely exceed 12,000 rpm during qualifying and race, due to the new fuel flow restrictions.[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-82)

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Mercedes-AMG_F1_M10_EQ_Power%2B_at_Formula_1_Exhibition%2C_London.jpg/250px-Mercedes-AMG_F1_M10_EQ_Power%2B_at_Formula_1_Exhibition%2C_London.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Mercedes-AMG_F1_M10_EQ_Power%2B_at_Formula_1_Exhibition,_London.jpg)The M10 EQ Power+ that powered the [Mercedes AMG F1 W10 EQ Power+](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercedes_AMG_F1_W10_EQ_Power%2B "Mercedes AMG F1 W10 EQ Power+") which took both championships in [2019](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019_Formula_One_World_Championship "2019 Formula One World Championship")

Energy recovery systems such as KERS had a boost of 160 hp (120 kW) and 2 megajoules per lap. KERS was renamed Motor Generator Unit–Kinetic (MGU-K). [Heat energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_energy "Heat energy") recovery systems were also allowed, under the name Motor Generator Unit–Heat (MGU-H). 
The 2015 season was an improvement on 2014, adding about 30–50 hp (20–40 kW) to most engines, the Mercedes engine being the most powerful with 870 hp (649 kW). In 2019, Renault's engine was claimed to have hit 1,000 hp in qualifying trim.[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-83)

Of the previous manufacturers, only Mercedes, Ferrari and Renault produced engines to the new formula in 2014, whereas Cosworth stopped supplying engines. Honda returned as an engine manufacturer in 2015, with McLaren switching to Honda power after using the Mercedes engine in 2014. 
In January 2018, the FIA issued a technical directive to prevent engine manufacturers from supplying customer teams with unequal engines, ensuring engine performance parity with works teams.[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-84)[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-85)[[86]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-86)

In 2019, Red Bull switched from using a Renault engine to Honda power. Honda supplied both Red Bull and AlphaTauri. Honda withdrew as a power unit supplier at the end of [2021](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Formula_One_World_Championship "2021 Formula One World Championship"), with Red Bull taking over the project and producing the engine [in-house](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Bull_Powertrains "Red Bull Powertrains").[[87]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-87)

In 2017, the FIA began negotiations with existing constructors and potential new manufacturers over the next generation of engines with a projected introduction date of [2021](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Formula_One_World_Championship "2021 Formula One World Championship") but delayed to [2022](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2022_Formula_One_World_Championship "2022 Formula One World Championship") due to the effects of the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-88) The initial proposal was designed to simplify engine designs, cut costs, promote new entries and address criticisms directed at the 2014 generation of engines. It called for the 1.6 L V6 configuration to

criticisms directed at the 2014 generation of engines. It called for the 1.6 L V6 configuration to be retained, but abandoned the complex Motor Generator Unit–Heat (MGU-H) system.[[89]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-2021_plans-89) The [Motor Generator Unit–Kinetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinetic_energy_recovery_system "Kinetic energy recovery system") (MGU-K) would be more powerful, with a greater emphasis on driver deployment and a more flexible introduction to allow for tactical use. The proposal also called for the introduction of standardised components and design parameters to make components produced by all manufacturers compatible with one another in a system dubbed "plug in and

produced by all manufacturers compatible with one another in a system dubbed "plug in and play".[[89]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-2021_plans-89) A further proposal to allow four-wheel drive cars was also made, with the front axle driven by an MGU-K unit—as opposed to the traditional driveshaft—that functioned independently of the MGU-K providing power to the rear axle, mirroring the system developed by [Porsche](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") for the [919 Hybrid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porsche_919_Hybrid "Porsche 919 Hybrid") race car.[[90]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-P919-90)[[91]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-91)

However, mostly due to no new engine supplier applying for F1 entry in 2021 and 2022, the abolishment of MGU-H, a more powerful MGU-K and a four-wheel drive system were all shelved with the possibility of their re-introduction for 2026. Instead, the teams and [FIA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FIA "FIA") agreed to a radical change in body/chassis aerodynamics to promote more battles on the course at closer distances to each other. They further agreed to an increase in alcohol content from 5.75% to 10% of fuel, and to implement a freeze on power unit design for 2022–2025, with the internal combustion engine (ICE), turbocharger and MGU-H being frozen on 1 March and the energy store, MGU-K and control electronics being frozen on 1 September during the 2022

and the energy store, MGU-K and control electronics being frozen on 1 September during the 2022 season.[[92]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-92) [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_in_Formula_One "Honda in Formula One"), the outgoing engine supplier in 2021, was keen to keep the MGU-H, and [Red Bull](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Bull_Powertrains "Red Bull Powertrains"), who took over the engine production project, backed that opinion.[[93]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-93) The 4WD system was planned to be based on Porsche 919 Hybrid system,[[90]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-P919-90) but Porsche ended up not becoming an F1 engine supplier for 2021–2022.

### 2026–2030
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2026–2030")]

New engine regulations were introduced for the [2026](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Formula_One_World_Championship "2026 Formula One World Championship") season. These engine regulations retain the turbocharged 1.6 V6 internal combustion engine configuration used since 2014. The new power units produce over 1,000 [bhp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horsepower#Brake_horsepower "Horsepower") (750 kW), although with a greatly increased reliance on electrical energy, resulting in an approximately 50-50 split between combustion and electrical power.[[94]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-94) The costly and complex [MGU-H](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antilag_system#MGU-H "Antilag system") (Motor Generator Unit – Heat) has been removed, while the MGU-K's (Motor

"Antilag system") (Motor Generator Unit – Heat) has been removed, while the MGU-K's (Motor Generator Unit – Kinetic) output has been increased to 470 bhp (350 [kW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt")) from 160 bhp (120 [kW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt")). The power output of the internal combustion part of the power unit has decreased to 540 bhp (400 [kW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt")) from 850 [bhp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horsepower#Brake_horsepower "Horsepower") (630 kW). In addition, fuel flow rates have been limited based on theoretical energy available from rule-specified unified composition, rather than mass of the various fuel from different sources. There is also intended to be further restrictions on components such

fuel from different sources. There is also intended to be further restrictions on components such as MGU-Ks and exhausts imposed from 2027. The power units are run on a fully sustainable fuel, developed by the FIA.[[95]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-95)[[96]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-96)

[Audi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Audi "Audi") became a new engine supplier in 2026 along with their team's entry to the F1 grid.[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-97) [Ford](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford_Performance "Ford Performance") began a partnership with Red Bull Powertrains as [Red Bull Ford Powertrains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Bull_Ford_Powertrains "Red Bull Ford Powertrains") from 2026 after a 21-year absence from Formula One.[[98]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-98)[[99]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-99)[[100]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-100) Honda, under its subsidiary [Honda Racing

Honda, under its subsidiary [Honda Racing Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_Racing_Corporation "Honda Racing Corporation"), has also entered as a manufacturer for 2026 according to the FIA after officially leaving the sport in 2021.[[101]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-101) The FIA also confirmed that Ferrari, Mercedes-AMG and Alpine (Renault) were registered as power unit suppliers for 2026.[[102]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-102) However, on 30 September 2024, owing to lack of strong results with its power unit during the V6 turbo-hybrid era since it began in 2014, [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_in_Formula_One "Renault in Formula One") announced it would be ending its engine programme following

"Renault in Formula One") announced it would be ending its engine programme following the conclusion of the [2025](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Formula_One_World_Championship "2025 Formula One World Championship") championship and would not be making engines for the new 2026 regulations after all.[[103]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-103) A 2025 FIA proposal to end the 2026 engine formula for 2028 and switch to sustainably fuelled naturally aspirated V10 engines was rejected by teams.[[104]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-104) On 23 April 2025, the FIA confirmed that General Motors through its Cadillac brand would become a power unit supplier from the 2029 season onwards. The [Cadillac Formula One

brand would become a power unit supplier from the 2029 season onwards. The [Cadillac Formula One Team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cadillac_in_Formula_One "Cadillac in Formula One") were already announced as joining the grid from the 2026 season onwards using customer Ferrari power units for an interim period whilst the Cadillac engine is developed.[[105]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-105)

#### 2027
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2027")]

Criticisms about the 2026 regulations were raised with the FIA following observations that the engine has become over-reliant on energy management, with the cars losing speed on long straights due to "super clipping", and abnormal differences with their closing speeds while energy is deployed compared to harvesting it.[[106]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-106)[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-electrical_boost-107) After tweaks were made before the Miami Grand Prix, inclucing an increase to the "super clipping" limit from 340 bhp (250 kW) to 470 bhp (350 kW),[[108]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-108)[[109]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-109) the FIA decided the week

the FIA decided the week after the race that Formula One would move away from the current 50-50 split between combustion and electrical power to a provisional 60/40 split, with the ICE's power increasing by 67 bhp (50 [kW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt")) through fuel flow rate increase. Further tweaks are planned before the regulation changes are ratified.[[110]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-110)

#### Combustion, construction, operation, power and fuel
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Combustion, construction, operation, power and fuel")]

* **Manufacturers** : [Mercedes-Benz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mercedes_AMG_High_Performance_Powertrains "Mercedes AMG High Performance Powertrains"), [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_Sport_F1 "Renault Sport F1") (including [TAG Heuer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TAG_Heuer "TAG Heuer") rebadging until 2018), [Ferrari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scuderia_Ferrari "Scuderia Ferrari") and [Red Bull Powertrains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Bull_Powertrains "Red Bull Powertrains") ([Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda_in_Formula_One "Honda in Formula One"))

* **Type** : Hybrid-powered 4-stroke piston. '4-stroke' may imply Otto-cycle, but it is not required. [Atkinson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atkinson_cycle "Atkinson cycle")/[Miller cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miller_cycle "Miller cycle") allowed.
  * **Configuration** : [V6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/V6_engine "V6 engine") [hybrid turbocharged](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hybrid_turbocharger "Hybrid turbocharger") engine
  * **V-angle** : 90° cylinder angle
  * **Displacement** : 1.6 [litres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Litre "Litre") (98 [cubic inches](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubic_inch "Cubic inch"))
  * **Bore** : 80 [mm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Millimetre "Millimetre") (3.150 [in](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inch "Inch"))

* **Stroke** : 53 [mm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Millimetre "Millimetre") (2.087 [in](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inch "Inch"))
  * **Compression ratio** : Max. 18:1
  * **Valvetrain** : [DOHC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overhead_camshaft "Overhead camshaft"), 24-valve (four valves per cylinder)

* **Fuel** : Minimum 87 (RON+MON)/2 unleaded petroleum + at least 10% "advanced sustainable" Ethanol. There are finer composition and property limitations, but improvements within the limits are allowed, provided a sample is submitted and approved by FIA. Honda, for example, requested and [ExxonMobil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ExxonMobil "ExxonMobil") agreed to provide special formulations with carbon-neutral components suited for lean/rapid-burn techniques.[[122]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-138)
  * **Fuel delivery** : [Petrol direct injection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Petrol_direct_injection "Petrol direct injection"), [port injection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manifold_injection "Manifold injection") allowed.

* **Maximum fuel injection pressure** : 500 [bar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bar_\(unit\) "Bar \(unit\)") (7,252 [psi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pound_per_square_inch "Pound per square inch"))
  * **Number of fuel injectors** : Max. 1 per cylinder. This is a severe limitation on [stratified charge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stratified_charge_engine "Stratified charge engine") and sub-chamber ignition used for lean and rapid burn, as separate injectors cannot be used for sub- and main chambers to provide rich and lean gas.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-HondaEff-15) The number of injections per cycle is not limited.
  * **Fuel flow rate limit** : (0.009 × rpm) + 5.5 up to 100 kg/h, with further limits under partial-throttle conditions.

* **Fuel use limit** : 110 kg / race
  * **Aspiration** : Single-[turbocharger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger") with in-line electric motor/generator (MGU-H)
  * **Power output** : About 850 + 161 [hp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horsepower "Horsepower") (634 + 120 [kW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt")) @ 10,500 rpm and higher
  * **Torque** : Approx. 600–815 [N⋅m](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton-metre "Newton-metre") (443–601 [lb⋅ft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pound-foot_\(torque\) "Pound-foot \(torque\)"))[[123]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-139)

* **Lubrication** : [Dry sump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dry_sump "Dry sump"). Oil may not contain fuel octane boosting additives. Oil consumption is limited to Max. 0.30L / 100 km. Fluid level in oil tank is monitored by telemetry.
  * **Maximum revs** : Unlimited (in practice, no engine goes much above 12,000 rpm as efficiency declines)

* **Engine management** : FIA Standard ECU. ECU (Electronic Control Unit) controls energy recovery and other chassis control / telemetry functions as well. Power unit control part of the ECU evolved from McLaren TAG-320B of 2019. Most parts of the program to operate the ECU were free to be improved as long as their copies (versions) submitted, registered, and approved by FIA (there were limitations on the number of versions in a season), but Power Unit control part of the program is frozen mostly from 2022 to 2025 seasons.

* **Max. speed** : Approximately 370 [km/h](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilometres_per_hour "Kilometres per hour") (230 [mph](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miles_per_hour "Miles per hour")) (Monza, Baku and Mexico); 340 [km/h](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilometres_per_hour "Kilometres per hour") (211 [mph](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miles_per_hour "Miles per hour")) normal tracks
  * **Mass** : Minimum 150 [kg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram") (330.69 [lb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pound_\(mass\) "Pound \(mass\)")) complete
  * **Cooling** : Single water pump
  * **Ignition** : No more than 5 sparks per cycle
  * **Exhaust systems** : Single exhaust with central exit

##### Forced induction
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Forced induction")]
  * **Turbocharger mass** : 8 [kg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram") (17.6 [lb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pound_\(mass\) "Pound \(mass\)")) depending on the turbine housing used
  * **Turbocharger rev limit** : 125,000 rpm
  * **Pressure charging** : Single-stage compressor and exhaust turbine, common-shaft with MGU-H
  * **Turbo boost pressure** : Unlimited but typically 400–500 kPa (4.0–5.0 bar) absolute

* **Turbo boost pressure** : Unlimited but typically 400–500 kPa (4.0–5.0 bar) absolute
  * **Wastegate** : Maximum of two pop-off and two wastegate valves, electronic- or pneumatic-controlled. (Pop-off valves limit the intake pressure by recirculating the charge. Wastegate valves release excess pressure to the atmosphere.)

##### [ERS systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_recovery_system "Energy recovery system")
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: ERS systems")]
  * **[MGU-K](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MGU-K "MGU-K") RPM**: Max. 50,000 rpm, fixed driven/drive ratio by/to the crankshaft
  * **MGU-K power** : Max. 120 kW (160 [bhp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horsepower#Brake_horsepower "Horsepower"))
  * **Energy recovered by MGU-K** : Max. 2 MJ (0.56 [kWh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt-hour "Kilowatt-hour")) / lap

* **Energy received by MGU-K** : Max. 4 MJ (1.1 [kWh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt-hour "Kilowatt-hour")) / lap from Energy Store. A combination of high-voltage [lithium-ion battery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery "Lithium-ion battery") with a series of [Super Capacitors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supercapacitor "Supercapacitor") acting as the buffer for high charge/discharge current. Max. voltage: 1000 V, weight limit: Minimum 20 kg. Maximum 25 kg. Unlimited from MGU-H.[[a]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-MGUH-140)
  * **[MGU-H](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MGU-H "MGU-H") RPM**: Same as the turbocharger speed. Max. 125,000 rpm

* **Energy recovered by MGU-H** : Unlimited[[a]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_note-MGUH-140)
  * **Energy released by MGU-H to drive the turbocharger or MGU-K:** Unlimited.

#### Spec notes
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_One_engines&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Spec notes")]
  1. ^ [_**a**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_ref-MGUH_140-0) [_**b**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_engines#cite_ref-MGUH_140-1) Due to these rules, the turbocharger is often used not to increase intake pressure (i.e., with pop-off valve open), but to drive MGU-H which provides electricity so that MGU-K output is pegged at 120kW for maximum acceleration regardless of the engine speed.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

The **Indian Grand Prix** was a Formula One race in the calendar of the [FIA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") [Formula One](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") World Championship, which was held at the [Buddh International Circuit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddh_International_Circuit "Buddh International Circuit") in sector 25 along [Yamuna Expressway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yamuna_Expressway "Yamuna Expressway") in [Gautam Buddh Nagar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gautam_Buddh_Nagar "Gautam Buddh Nagar") district of [Uttar Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") from 2011 until 2013.

The [first edition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_Indian_Grand_Prix "2011 Indian Grand Prix") took place on 30 October 2011, as the 17th race of the [2011 Formula One season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_FIA_Formula_One_World_Championship "2011 FIA Formula One World Championship").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-1) The inaugural race was won by Germany's [Sebastian Vettel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sebastian_Vettel "Sebastian Vettel"). Jaypee Sports International Limited was the organizer of Formula One racing in India and had signed a five-year contract with Formula One Management (FOM) to host the championship in India.

In August 2013, the FIA announced that the Indian Grand Prix would miss the 2014 edition before returning to an early-season slot in 2015. Subsequently, due to a tax dispute with the Uttar Pradesh government, the 2015 edition was cancelled and its possible return delayed until 2016.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-2015_cancelled-2) However, no resolution was found and the Grand Prix has not since been restored to the calendar.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-3)
## History
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

As early as 1997, there were plans to host an Indian Grand Prix at [Calcutta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-rediff-4) In 2003, India had only two permanent raceways, one in [Chennai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") ([Irungattukottai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Irungattukottai_Race_Track "Irungattukottai Race Track")), and the [Kari Motor Speedway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kari_Motor_Speedway "Kari Motor Speedway") in [Coimbatore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coimbatore "Coimbatore").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-5) At that time two 600-acre (240 ha) sites in the vicinity of the [Bangalore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore")

ha) sites in the vicinity of the [Bangalore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bangalore "Bangalore") airport were examined. Also, in the state of [Andhra Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh"), Chief Minister [Chandrababu Naidu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/N._Chandrababu_Naidu "N. Chandrababu Naidu") reserved 1,500 acres (610 ha) of land near the airport at [Hyderabad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyderabad,_India "Hyderabad, India"). Vicky Chandhok, father of [Karun Chandhok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karun_Chandhok "Karun Chandhok"), stated in an interview: "_Andhra Pradesh is really pushing it like no other state! It is great to see a Chief Minister pushing so hard. Bangalore is a great location, mainly because of the weather_

see a Chief Minister pushing so hard. Bangalore is a great location, mainly because of the weather_ ".[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-rediff-4) In December 2003, a seven-year pre-agreement to host the GP in Hyderabad in 2007 was signed. The track was to be built near Gopanapally village, near the outskirts of Hyderabad, and consisted of 1,367 acres (553 ha) of land.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-timesofindia-6)

However, in 2004 competition arose from [Mumbai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), to change the location from Hyderabad to Mumbai.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-7) Formula One commercial rights holder [Bernie Ecclestone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Ecclestone "Bernie Ecclestone") expected India to host a Grand Prix within three years, aiming for locating at either Hyderabad or Mumbai.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-8) In the end these projects were never realised, possibly owing to anti-tobacco legislation,[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-timesofindia-6) and a change in government

and a change in government policy.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-9)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-10) Both projects then were declared "dead" in the second half of 2004, when Mumbai's government decided "not to waste money on car fumes while there are more serious issues",[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-11) and the Hyderabad location was converted to an IT park for technology companies.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-12) However, sites at Mumbai were still being investigated (Gorai and Navi Mumbai). In 2005, [Narain Karthikeyan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Narain_Karthikeyan "Narain Karthikeyan") was due to demonstrate a [Jordan Grand

"Narain Karthikeyan") was due to demonstrate a [Jordan Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jordan_Grand_Prix "Jordan Grand Prix") car in Mumbai, but the road proved to be too bumpy.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-13)

In 2007, five locations remained in the running for hosting the Indian Grand Prix: Bangalore; the [Gurgaon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gurgaon "Gurgaon") district in the state of [Haryana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"); a permanent track somewhere near New Delhi; a [street circuit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Street_circuit "Street circuit") in New Delhi, as proposed by [Vijay Mallya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vijay_Mallya "Vijay Mallya");[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-14)[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-15) and a site in [Lucknow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow"), Uttar Pradesh.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-16)

Following months of negotiations, the [Indian Olympic Association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Olympic_Association "Indian Olympic Association") (IOA) and Ecclestone announced in June 2007 a provisional agreement for India to host its first [Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grand_Prix_motor_racing "Grand Prix motor racing") as a part of the 2009 Formula One season.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-PlanetF1-17)[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-18) The track would be built in [Gurgaon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gurgaon "Gurgaon"), in consultation with architect [Hermann Tilke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermann_Tilke "Hermann

in consultation with architect [Hermann Tilke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermann_Tilke "Hermann Tilke").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-rediff-4)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-20)

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Massa_Indiangrandprix.jpg/250px-Massa_Indiangrandprix.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Massa_Indiangrandprix.jpg)[Felipe Massa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Felipe_Massa "Felipe Massa") at Indian Grand Prix 2013

However, in September 2007 it was announced that the debut race would take place in 2010 on the [Buddh International Circuit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddh_International_Circuit "Buddh International Circuit") in [Greater Noida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greater_Noida "Greater Noida").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-21)[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-22) After further assessments of the timeframe involved, Ecclestone announced in September 2008 that the Indian Grand Prix had been delayed to 2011.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-23)

In October 2008, the [Renault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renault_in_Formula_One "Renault in Formula One") F1 team showcased their car on a tour across all the sites which were previously linked to a Formula One circuit in India, except Mumbai: Gurgaon, Lucknow, Bangalore, Hyderabad and Kolkata.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-24) In November 2008 they also performed a street demonstration on [Rajpath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajpath "Rajpath") at the [India Gate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India_Gate "India Gate") end, New Delhi; the car was driven by [Nelson Piquet Jr.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nelson_Piquet_Jr. "Nelson Piquet

was driven by [Nelson Piquet Jr.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nelson_Piquet_Jr. "Nelson Piquet Jr.")[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-25)[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-26) In August 2009, the [McLaren](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McLaren "McLaren") team showcased their car in Lucknow.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-27) On 11 October 2009, the [Red Bull Racing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Bull_Racing "Red Bull Racing") team gave a demonstration in Mumbai. [David Coulthard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Coulthard "David Coulthard") drove the Red Bull car across Mumbai's [Bandra–Worli Sea Link](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bandra%E2%80%93Worli_Sea_Link "Bandra–Worli Sea
Sea Link](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bandra%E2%80%93Worli_Sea_Link "Bandra–Worli Sea Link").[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-28)

The Indian Grand Prix had the potential to generate around $170 million in revenue and employ as many as 10,000 people.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-29)[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-30) The opportunity in advertising tie-ups between manufacturers and Formula One were also a consideration.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-2011debut-31) The race coincided with the important [Diwali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diwali "Diwali") holiday, when [consumer spending](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consumer_spending "Consumer spending") is typically at its highest and many people are traveling, which further improved economic prospects. It was one of the few F1 races at the time that were not

which further improved economic prospects. It was one of the few F1 races at the time that were not subsidized by government, and the venue had to pay $40 million per year to [Formula One Holdings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One_Holdings "Formula One Holdings") to host the event.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-32)

The Indian Grand Prix was set to have a one-year hiatus and then return on the [2015](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2015_Formula_One_World_Championship "2015 Formula One World Championship") calendar as confirmed by the FIA. Bernie Ecclestone later cast doubt on this, suggesting the race would be delayed until 2016,[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-2015_cancelled-2) and this doubt was later confirmed by Ecclestone in August 2014 in Belgium. The Indian Grand Prix was cancelled for the second consecutive year following tax disputes between the FIA and the Uttar Pradesh

for the second consecutive year following tax disputes between the FIA and the Uttar Pradesh government.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-33)[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-34)[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-35) The circuit, despite the placards saying "New Delhi"[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-36) is not located in the union territory of [Delhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), but in the neighbouring state of [Uttar Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh").[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-32) The government of Uttar Pradesh, led by the then Chief Minister [Akhilesh

The government of Uttar Pradesh, led by the then Chief Minister [Akhilesh Yadav](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akhilesh_Yadav "Akhilesh Yadav") said that F1 is not a sport, but entertainment and levied taxes on the event.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-32)

## Circuit
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Circuit")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Buddh_International_Circuit.svg/250px-Buddh_International_Circuit.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Buddh_International_Circuit.svg)"The Circuit Official Logo"
Main article: [Buddh International Circuit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddh_International_Circuit "Buddh International Circuit")

The race was held at the [Buddh International Circuit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddh_International_Circuit "Buddh International Circuit") in [Greater Noida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greater_Noida "Greater Noida") near New Delhi between 2011 and 2013. The 5.125 km circuit has been designed by German architect [Hermann Tilke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermann_Tilke "Hermann Tilke"). Four million cubic tons of earth were moved to achieve the rise and fall through the lap. The track is spread across an area of 875 acres,[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Grand_Prix#cite_note-37) and is a part of Jaypee Green Sports city.
